AYESHA
Ayesha
(Aisha bint Abi Bakr, c. 613–678 CE)
Ayesha (Aisha bint Abi Bakr) was one of the wives of the Prophet Muhammad and is
regarded as a central figure in early Islamic history. She is especially revered in Sunni Islam.
Key Points
Daughter of Abu Bakr, the first caliph after Muhammad.
Known for her intelligence, memory, and scholarship.
Narrated over 2,000 hadiths (sayings and actions of the Prophet), making her one of
the most important transmitters of Islamic tradition.
Played a political role after Muhammad’s death, including involvement in the Battle of
the Camel (656 CE) during the first Islamic civil war.
Remembered as a teacher, jurist, and authority in Islamic law.
Religious Significance
In Sunni Islam, she is highly honored and considered among the “Mothers of the
Believers.”
In Shia Islam, views are more complex due to her political opposition to Ali during the
early conflicts.

She Who Must Be Obeyed
“She Who Must Be Obeyed”
The phrase refers to Ayesha, the immortal queen in the novel She by H. Rider Haggard.
Who is she?
Ayesha is an ageless, powerful ruler of a hidden African kingdom.
She is called “She Who Must Be Obeyed” by her subjects.
She possesses beauty, intellect, and supernatural longevity (granted by a mystical
flame).
The story blends adventure, mysticism, lost-civilization lore, and Victorian
romanticism.
Why the phrase endured
The title became shorthand for a dominant, commanding woman.
It has been referenced widely in literature and pop culture.
It influenced later “lost world” fiction and exotic adventure genres.
If you’re drawing this phrase into a larger symbolic or narrative framework, I can explore it
through mythic, literary, psychological, or cultural lenses.

Xanadu
Refers to Xanadu, the summer capital of Kublai Khan.
In the poem, it becomes a dreamlike landscape of imagination, power, and
subconscious depth.
Alph (often spelled “Alf” in shorthand)
A mythic river, likely invented by Coleridge.
It flows through caverns “measureless to man” and descends to a “sunless sea.”

The creative unconscious
The life force
The stream of imagination
A sacred current beneath constructed civilization
The Deeper Tension
The pleasure-dome (order, empire, decree) stands above
The sacred river (mystery, depth, primal flow).

Where Alph, the sacred river, ran…”
